% Auto-generated by nnes_run.py. Do not edit by hand.
% Known-truth DGP cells: canonical_low_action, canonical_high_action

\section{Generated Validation Results}
This section is generated by \texttt{nnes\_run.py} from the \texttt{experiments.known\_truth} harness. Hard gates are enforced. The low-dimensional cell is a sanity check; the high-dimensional cell is the main NNES validation because it exercises the larger state and reward-feature setting where the neural estimator is meant to matter.

\subsection{Validation Design}
Both cells use 2,000 simulated individuals, 80 periods per individual, known transitions, three actions, homogeneous rewards, and the shared Type A/B/C counterfactual oracles. The high-dimensional cell is the primary stress test; the low-dimensional cell checks that the same implementation still behaves correctly in the easy benchmark.

\begin{table}[H]
\centering\small
\caption{NNES known-truth validation cells.}
\begin{tabular}{llrrrrr}
\toprule
Cell & Role & States & State dim. & Reward params & Obs. & Gates \\
\midrule
Low-dimensional & sanity check & 21 & 2 & 4 & 160,000 & 11/11 \\
High-dimensional & primary validation & 81 & 16 & 32 & 160,000 & 11/11 \\
\bottomrule
\end{tabular}
\end{table}

The high-dimensional DGP passes the pre-estimation checks before NNES is fit: feature rank is 32/32, the reward-feature condition number is 1.377, all 81 states are observed, and state-action coverage is 0.959.

\subsection{Fit Summary}

\begin{table}[H]
\centering\small
\caption{NNES fit summary.}
\begin{tabular}{lrrrr}
\toprule
Cell & Converged & L-BFGS-B iter. & Final V loss & Log likelihood \\
\midrule
\texttt{canonical\_low\_action} & true & 10 & 0.0004 & -174875.8 \\
\texttt{canonical\_high\_action} & true & 29 & 0.0299 & -160272.9 \\
\bottomrule
\end{tabular}
\end{table}

The easy cell is very tight. The high-dimensional cell is deliberately harder: reward, value, and Q errors are larger, but they remain within the hard gates.

\subsection{Main Recovery Numbers}
\begin{table}[H]

\caption{Core recovery metrics.}
\begin{tabular}{llrr}
\toprule
Metric & Gate & Low-dimensional & High-dimensional \\
\midrule
Parameter cosine similarity & $\geq 0.95$ & 0.9982 & 0.9912 \\

Reward RMSE & $\leq 0.08$ & 0.0102 & 0.0640 \\
Policy total variation & $\leq 0.03$ & 0.0056 & 0.0238 \\

Q RMSE & $\leq 0.20$ & 0.0234 & 0.1371 \\
\bottomrule

\end{table}

\subsection{Counterfactual Recovery}
Each counterfactual gate requires regret below 0.05. All three counterfactuals pass in both cells.

\begin{table}[H]
\centering\small
\caption{Counterfactual regret.}
\begin{tabular}{lrrl}
\toprule
Counterfactual & Low-dimensional & High-dimensional & Interpretation \\
\midrule
Type A & 0.0002 & 0.0049 & reward/state shift \\
Type B & 0.0003 & 0.0056 & transition change \\
Type C & 0.0001 & 0.0013 & action restriction \\
\bottomrule
\end{tabular}
\end{table}

Exact parameter vectors, standard errors, log-likelihoods, policy distances, and all gate values are written to \texttt{nnes\_results.json}. The PDF reports the decision-critical values rather than printing the full 32-parameter high-dimensional vector.
